questions.title: GATE | CS | 2021 | Set 1 | C Programming | Function | Question 58
questions.question_html:
Consider the following ANSI C function: 

int SimpleFunction(int Y[], int n, int x)
{
int total = Y[0], loopIndex;
for (loopIndex=1; loopIndex<=n-1; loopIndex++)
  total=x*total +Y[loopIndex];
return total;
}

Let Z be an array of 10 elements with Z[i]=1, for all i such that 0 <= i <= 9. The value returned by SimpleFunction(Z,10,2) is __________ .
questions.explanation:
- Given function computes = 2 ^(i+1) − 1, for every i value.
- Therefore, 2 ^10 − 1 = 1024-1 = 1023
questions.explanation_html:
• Given function computes = 2(i+1) − 1, for every i value.
  • Therefore, 210 − 1 = 1024-1 = 1023

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2018 | C Programming | Functions | Question 47
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C code. Assume that unsigned long int type length is 64 bits.

C 
unsigned long int fun(unsigned long int n) {
        unsigned long int i, j = 0, sum = 0;
        for( i = n; i > 1; i = i/2) j++;
        for( ; j > 1; j = j/2) sum++;
        return sum;
}

The value returned when we call fun with the input [Tex]2^{40}[/Tex] is
questions.explanation_html:
C 
// n takes 2^40
unsigned long int fun(unsigned long int n) {

        // initialized sum = 0 
        unsigned long int i, j = 0, sum = 0;
           
        //First it takes i = n = 2^40, 
        //then it divides i by 2 and incremented once j
        //each time, that's will make makes j = 40,
        for( i=n; i>1; i=i/2) j++; 

        //Now the value of j = 40,
        //it divides j by 2 and incremented once sum
        //each time, that's will make makes sum = 5,
        for( ; j>1; j=j/2) sum++;

        //returns sum = 5
        return sum;
}

So, answer is 5.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2018 | C Programming | Pointers | Question 52
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program:

C 
#include <stdio.h>
void fun1(char *s1, char *s2) {
  char *temp;
  temp = s1;
  s1 = s2;
  s2 = temp;
}
void fun2(char **s1, char **s2) {
  char *temp;
  temp = *s1;
  *s1 = *s2;
  *s2 = temp;
}
int main() {
  char *str1 = "Hi", *str2 = "Bye";
  fun1(str1, str2);
  printf("%s %s", str1, str2);
  fun2(&str1, &str2);
  printf("%s %s", str1, str2);
  return 0;
}

The output of the program above is
questions.explanation:
fun1(char *s1, char *s2) Above function scope is local, so the value changed here won't affect actual parameters. SO the values will be 'Hi Bye'.   fun2(char **s1, char **s2) In this function value is pointer to pointer, so it changes pointer of the actual value. So values will be 'Bye Hi'
Answer is 'Hi Bye Bye Hi'
Option (A) is correct.
questions.explanation_html:
fun1(char *s1, char *s2) Above function scope is local, so the value changed here won't affect actual parameters. SO the values will be 'Hi Bye'.   fun2(char **s1, char **s2) In this function value is pointer to pointer, so it changes pointer of the actual value. So values will be 'Bye Hi'

Answer is 'Hi Bye Bye Hi'

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2020 | C Programming | Functions | Question 58
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C functions.

C 
int tob (int b, int* arr) {
    int i;
    for (i = 0; b>0; i++)  {
        if (b%2)  arr [i] = 1;
        else      arr[i] = 0;
        b = b/2;
    }
    return (i);
}
 

int pp(int a, int b)  {
    int  arr[20];
    int i, tot = 1, ex, len;
    ex = a;
    len = tob(b, arr);
    for (i=0; i<len ; i++) {
         if (arr[i] ==1)
             tot = tot * ex;
         ex= ex*ex;
    }
return (tot) ;
}

The value returned by pp(3,4) is ________ .

Note -

This question was Numerical Type.
questions.explanation:
The function pp(3, 4) effectively computes 3 ^4 using binary exponentiation.
First, tob(b, arr) converts b = 4 to binary and stores it in arr (least significant bit at index 0):
For b = 4 :
- Iteration 0: b = 4 → 4 % 2 = 0 → arr[0] = 0 , b = 4 / 2 = 2
- Iteration 1: b = 2 → 2 % 2 = 0 → arr[1] = 0 , b = 2 / 2 = 1
- Iteration 2: b = 1 → 1 % 2 = 1 → arr[2] = 1 , b = 1 / 2 = 0 → loop ends
So after len = tob(4, arr) :
- len = 3
- arr[0] = 0 , arr[1] = 0 , arr[2] = 1 (4 in binary is 100)
Now pp(3, 4) runs this loop:
- Initial values: tot = 1 , ex = 3
Iteration 1 (i = 0):
- arr[0] = 0 → tot unchanged → tot = 1
- ex = ex * ex = 3 * 3 = 9
Iteration 2 (i = 1):
- arr[1] = 0 → tot unchanged → tot = 1
- ex = ex * ex = 9 * 9 = 81
Iteration 3 (i = 2):
- arr[2] = 1 → tot = tot * ex = 1 * 81 = 81
- ex is squared again, but not used afterward.
Thus, the value returned by pp(3, 4) is 81 .
questions.explanation_html:
The function pp(3, 4) effectively computes 34 using binary exponentiation.

First, tob(b, arr) converts b = 4 to binary and stores it in arr (least significant bit at index 0):

For b = 4:

  • Iteration 0: b = 4 → 4 % 2 = 0 → arr[0] = 0, b = 4 / 2 = 2
  • Iteration 1: b = 2 → 2 % 2 = 0 → arr[1] = 0, b = 2 / 2 = 1
  • Iteration 2: b = 1 → 1 % 2 = 1 → arr[2] = 1, b = 1 / 2 = 0 → loop ends

So after len = tob(4, arr):

  • len = 3
  • arr[0] = 0, arr[1] = 0, arr[2] = 1 (4 in binary is 100)

Now pp(3, 4) runs this loop:

  • Initial values: tot = 1, ex = 3

Iteration 1 (i = 0):

  • arr[0] = 0 → tot unchanged → tot = 1
  • ex = ex * ex = 3 * 3 = 9

Iteration 2 (i = 1):

  • arr[1] = 0 → tot unchanged → tot = 1
  • ex = ex * ex = 9 * 9 = 81

Iteration 3 (i = 2):

  • arr[2] = 1 → tot = tot * ex = 1 * 81 = 81
  • ex is squared again, but not used afterward.

Thus, the value returned by pp(3, 4) is 81.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2021 | Set 1 | C Programming | Loops | Question 47
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program.
 

C 
#include <stdio.h>

int main()
{
    int i, j, count;
    count = 0;
    i = 0;
    
    for (j = -3; j <= 3; j++)
    {
        if ((j >= 0) && (i++))
        {
            count = count + j;
        }
    }
    
    count = count + i;
    printf("%d", count);
    
    return 0;
}

Which one of the following options is correct?

options.text: The program will not compile successfully
options.html: The program will not compile successfully

options.text: The program will compile successfully and output 10 when executed
options.html: The program will compile successfully and output 10 when executed

options.text: The program will compile successfully and output 8 when executed
options.html: The program will compile successfully and output 8 when executed

options.text: The program will compile successfully and output 13 when executed
options.html: The program will compile successfully and output 13 when executed

questions.explanation:
The program will compile successfully and output 10 when executed.
Variables:
- i=0
- j will iterate from −3 to 3 (inclusive).
- count=0.
- Post-increment i: The current value of i is used first, then i is incremented.
- The condition checks if i (before increment) is nonzero.
Update for each j:
- j=1: count=0+1=1, i=2.
- j=2: count=1+2=3,i=3.
- j=3: count=3+3=6,i=4.
After the loop:
count=count+i=6+4=10.
questions.explanation_html:
The program will compile successfully and output 10 when executed.

Variables:

  • i=0
  • j will iterate from −3 to 3 (inclusive).
  • count=0.
  • Post-increment i: The current value of i is used first, then i is incremented.
  • The condition checks if i (before increment) is nonzero.

Update for each j:

  • j=1: count=0+1=1, i=2.
  • j=2: count=1+2=3,i=3.
  • j=3: count=3+3=6,i=4.

After the loop:

count=count+i=6+4=10.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2021 | Set 2 | C Programming | Loops & Conditionals | Question 20
questions.question_html:
Consider the following ANSI C program. 

#include < stdio.h >
int main( ) 
{
    int arr[4][5];
    int  i, j;
    for (i=0; i<4; i++) 
    {
        for (j=0; j<5; j++) 
        {
            arr[i][j] = 10 * i + j;
        }
    }
    printf("%d", *(arr[1]+9));
    return 0;
}

What is the output of the above program?
questions.explanation:
The program initializes a 2D array arr of size 4×54 : arr[4][5]
The nested loop assigns values to the array: arr [i][j] = 10⋅i+j
For each i and j, the values in the array are: arr[0][4]=4, arr[1][4]=14, arr[2][4]=24, arr[3][4]=34
The key part of the code is the expression:
arr[1] : Points to the first element of the second row, arr[1][0].
arr[1]+9 : Adds an offset of 9 elements to the base address of arr[1].
Value of arr[2][4]=10⋅2+4=24 (Final Answer)

The nested loop assigns values to the array: arr [i][j] = 10⋅i+j

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2021 | Set 2 | C Programming | Functions | Question 33
questions.question_html:
Consider the following ANSI C function: 

int SomeFunction (int x, int y)
{
    if ((x==1) || (y==1)) return 1;
    if (x==y) return x;
    if (x > y) return SomeFunction(x-y, y);
    if (y > x) return SomeFunction (x, y-x);
 
}

The value returned by SomeFunction (15, 255) is __________ .
questions.explanation:
This function is calculating the GCD of the two numbers by repeated subtraction.
Let’s trace the recursive calls:
- Initial Call : SomeFunction (15,255)
- Since 15<25515 < 25515<255, it goes to: SomeFunction (15,255−15) = SomeFunction (15,240)
- Second Call : SomeFunction(15,240)
- Since 15<24015 < 24015<240, it goes to: SomeFunction (15,240−15) = SomeFunction (15,225)
Continue Subtracting : On repeating the subtraction process we will get:
SomeFunction(15,225)→SomeFunction(15,210)→SomeFunction(15,195)→⋯→SomeFunction(15,15)
Base Case : When x==y, SomeFunction(15,15) returns 15
The GCD of 15 and 255 is 15, which is the value returned by the function.
questions.explanation_html:
This function is calculating the GCD of the two numbers by repeated subtraction.

Let’s trace the recursive calls:

  • Initial Call: SomeFunction (15,255)
  • Since 15<25515 < 25515<255, it goes to: SomeFunction (15,255−15) = SomeFunction (15,240)
  • Second Call: SomeFunction(15,240)
  • Since 15<24015 < 24015<240, it goes to: SomeFunction (15,240−15) = SomeFunction (15,225)

Continue Subtracting: On repeating the subtraction process we will get:

SomeFunction(15,225)→SomeFunction(15,210)→SomeFunction(15,195)→⋯→SomeFunction(15,15)

Base Case: When x==y, SomeFunction(15,15) returns 15

The GCD of 15 and 255 is 15, which is the value returned by the function.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2021 | Set 2 | C Programming | Recursion | Question 59
questions.question_html:
Consider the following ANSI C program 

#include 
int foo(int x, int y, int q) 
    {
        if ((x<=0) && (y<=0))
        return q;
        if (x<=0)
        return foo(x, y-q, q);
        if (y<=0)
        return foo(x-q, y, q);
        return foo(x, y-q, q) + foo(x-q, y, q);
    }
int main( )
{
    int r = foo(15, 15, 10);
    printf(“%d”, r);
    return 0;
}

The output of the program upon execution is _________ .
questions.explanation:
- The function foo(x,y,q) recursively reduces x and y by q, splitting the problem into smaller subproblems until both x≤0 and y≤0, where it returns q.
- For foo(15,15,10), the recursion splits into two calls: foo(15,5,10) and foo(5,15,10), which further split into smaller cases.
- Each recursive call either adds q or terminates when x≤0 or y≤0, ensuring all paths eventually contribute to the total.
- Combining results, foo(15,15,10) adds up all contributions and returns 60.
questions.explanation_html:
• The function foo(x,y,q) recursively reduces x and y by q, splitting the problem into smaller subproblems until both x≤0 and y≤0, where it returns q.
  • For foo(15,15,10), the recursion splits into two calls: foo(15,5,10) and foo(5,15,10), which further split into smaller cases.
  • Each recursive call either adds q or terminates when x≤0 or y≤0, ensuring all paths eventually contribute to the total.
  • Combining results, foo(15,15,10) adds up all contributions and returns 60.

questions.question_html:
Consider the C program below. 
 

C 
#include <stdio.h>
int *A, stkTop;
int stkFunc (int opcode, int val)
{
    static int size=0, stkTop=0;
    switch (opcode)
    {
    case -1:
        size = val;
        break;
    case 0:
        if (stkTop < size ) A[stkTop++]=val;
        break;
    default:
        if (stkTop) return A[--stkTop];
    }
    return -1;
}
int main()
{
    int B[20];
    A=B;
    stkTop = -1;
    stkFunc (-1, 10);
    stkFunc (0, 5);
    stkFunc (0, 10);
    printf ("%d\n", stkFunc(1, 0)+ stkFunc(1, 0));
}

The value printed by the above program is ___________
questions.explanation:
The code in main, basically initializes a stack of size 10, then pushes 5, then pushes 10.
Finally the printf statement prints sum of two pop operations which is 10 + 5 = 15.
stkFunc (-1, 10); // Initialize size as 10
stkFunc (0, 5); // push 5
stkFunc (0, 10); // push 10
// print sum of two pop
printf ("%d\n", stkFunc(1, 0) + stkFunc(1, 0));
questions.explanation_html:
The code in main, basically initializes a stack of size 10, then pushes 5, then pushes 10. 

Finally the printf statement prints sum of two pop operations which is 10 + 5 = 15.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2017 | Set 2 | C Programming | Functions | Question 47
questions.question_html:
Consider the following snippet of a C program. Assume that swap(&x, &y) exchanges the contents of x and y. 

int main()
{
    int array[] = {3, 5, 1, 4, 6, 2};
    int done = 0;
    int i;

    while (done == 0)
    {
        done  = 1;
        for (i = 0; i <= 4; i++)
        {
            if (array[i] < array[i+1])
            {
                swap(&array[i], &array[i+1]);
                done = 0;
            }
        }
        for (i = 5; i >= 1; i--)
        {
            if (array[i] > array[i-1])
            {
                swap(&array[i], &array[i-1]);
                done = 0;
            }
        }
    }

    printf("%d", array[3]);
}

The output of the program is _____. Note: This question appeared as Numerical Answer Type.
questions.explanation_html:
After the execution of the for loop, the content of array:

First time:

5 3 4 6 2 1

Second time:

6 5 4 3 2 1

Third time:

6 5 4 3 2 1 Now, when while loop execute again, done = 1 and first and second for loop if condition will not be satisfied . Therefore, At the end,

value of

arr[3] = 3, Option C is correct.

CPP

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2015 | Set 2 | C Programming | Recursion | Question 9
questions.question_html:
Consider the following function written in the C programming language. The output of the above function on input “ABCD EFGH” is

C 
void foo (char *a)
{
  if (*a && *a != ` `)
  {
     foo(a+1);
     putchar(*a);
  }
}
questions.explanation:
Code Analysis:
- Base Condition : - The recursion stops when either: - ∗a* is \0 (end of the string), or
- ∗a is a space character ( ' ' ).
- Recursive Call : - The function calls foo(a+1), processing the next character in the string.
- Post-Recursive Action : - putchar(∗a) prints the current character after the recursive call returns . This effectively reverses the string segment that is processed.
The input string is: " ABCD EFGH " Step-by-Step Execution Segment: "ABCD" - Process the first character ′′A′: Call foo("BCD").
- Process ′B′: foo("CD").
- Process ′C′: Call foo("D").
- Process ′D′: Call foo(′\0′).
- Stop recursion at ′\0′ and start returning: Print ′D′, then ′C′, then ′B′, and finally ′A′.
Segment: "EFGH" - Key Point : The recursion stops at the space character ( ' ' ) because of the condition *a != ' '.
- The function does not process any characters after encountering the space. Therefore, "EFGH" is completely ignored.
Output The only processed segment is "ABCD" , which is printed in reverse: DCBA.
questions.explanation_html:
Code Analysis:

  • Base Condition:
    • The recursion stops when either:
      • ∗a* is \0 (end of the string), or
      • ∗a is a space character (' ').
  • Recursive Call:
    • The function calls foo(a+1), processing the next character in the string.
  • Post-Recursive Action:
    • putchar(∗a) prints the current character after the recursive call returns. This effectively reverses the string segment that is processed.
The input string is: "ABCD EFGH"

Step-by-Step Execution

Segment: "ABCD"

  1. Process the first character ′′A′: Call foo("BCD").
  2. Process ′B′: foo("CD").
  3. Process ′C′: Call foo("D").
  4. Process ′D′: Call foo(′\0′).
  5. Stop recursion at ′\0′ and start returning: Print ′D′, then ′C′, then ′B′, and finally ′A′.

Segment: "EFGH"

  • Key Point: The recursion stops at the space character (' ') because of the condition *a != ' '.
  • The function does not process any characters after encountering the space. Therefore, "EFGH" is completely ignored.

Output

The only processed segment is "ABCD", which is printed in reverse: DCBA.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2004 | C Programming | Function | Question 2
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C function

C 
void swap (int a, int b)
{
   int temp;
   temp = a;
   a = b;
   b = temp;
}

In order to exchange the values of two variables x and y.

options.text: swap(x,y) cannot be used as it does not return any value
options.html: swap(x,y) cannot be used as it does not return any value

options.text: swap(x,y) cannot be used as the parameters are passed by value
options.html: swap(x,y) cannot be used as the parameters are passed by value

questions.explanation:
The correct option is (D) "swap(x,y) cannot be used as the parameters are passed by value."
Explanation:
In C, when you pass parameters to a function, they are passed by value by default. It means that the function receives copies of the values of the parameters, not the actual variables themselves. Therefore, any changes made to the parameters inside the function do not affect the original variables in the calling function.
In the given function `swap(int a, int b)`, although it appears to swap the values of `a` and `b`, it actually swaps the copies of the values passed to it, not the original variables. So, calling `swap(x, y)` won't achieve the desired result of swapping the values of `x` and `y`.
To achieve the desired behavior, you need to pass the addresses of `x` and `y` to the function, so the function can directly modify the values at those memory locations. This is done by passing pointers to `x` and `y` as arguments. Hence, option (B) "Call swap (&x, &y)" is the correct way to use the `swap` function to exchange the values of `x` and `y`.

The correct option is (D) "swap(x,y) cannot be used as the parameters are passed by value."

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2016 | Set 2 | C programming | Recursion |  Question 47
questions.question_html:
Consider the following program:

C 
int f(int *p, int n)
{
    if (n <= 1) return 0;
    else return max(f(p+1,n-1),p[0]-p[1]);
}
int main()
{
    int a[] = {3,5,2,6,4};
    printf("%d", f(a,5));
}

Note: max(x,y) returns the maximum of x and y. The value printed by this program is
questions.explanation:
Look at the recursion stack of the given code in the below image. Assuming that the base address of array starts from 1000 and an integer takes 4 Bytes.
Step-by-Step Execution Step 1: Understand the Recursive Logic The function f(p,n) computes the maximum difference between adjacent elements in the array using recursion:
f(p,n) = max(f(p+1,n−1),p[0]−p[1]).
- Base Case : If n≤1n, return 0.
- Recursive Case : Compare: - The result of the recursive call f(p+1,n−1), which processes the rest of the array, and
- The difference between the first two elements, p[0]−p[1].
Step 2: Substitute Values for the Input Array: a={3,5,2,6,4}. Start with f(a,5):
- First Call : f(a,5) = max(f(a+1,4),3−5)=max(f(a+1,4),−2).
- Second Call : f(a+1,4)=max(f(a+2,3),5−2)=max(f(a+2,3),3).
- Third Call : f(a+2,3)=max(f(a+3,2),2−6)=max(f(a+3,2),−4).
- Fourth Call : f(a+3,2)=max(f(a+4,1),6−4)=max(0,2)=2 (base case for f(a+4,1).
Step 3: Backtrack to Get the Result - f(a+3,2)=2, so f(a+2,3)=max(2,−4)=2.
- f(a+2,3)=2, so f(a+1,4)=max(2,3)=3.
- f(a+1,4)=3, so f(a,5)=max(3,−2)=3.
Final Answer : The value printed by the program is: 3.
questions.explanation_html:
Look at the recursion stack of the given code in the below image. Assuming that the base address of array starts from 1000 and an integer takes 4 Bytes.

Step-by-Step Execution

Step 1: Understand the Recursive Logic

The function f(p,n) computes the maximum difference between adjacent elements in the array using recursion:

f(p,n) = max(f(p+1,n−1),p[0]−p[1]).

  • Base Case: If n≤1n, return 0.
  • Recursive Case: Compare:
    • The result of the recursive call f(p+1,n−1), which processes the rest of the array, and
    • The difference between the first two elements, p[0]−p[1].

Step 2: Substitute Values for the Input

Array: a={3,5,2,6,4}. Start with f(a,5):

  1. First Call: f(a,5) = max(f(a+1,4),3−5)=max(f(a+1,4),−2).
  2. Second Call: f(a+1,4)=max(f(a+2,3),5−2)=max(f(a+2,3),3).
  3. Third Call: f(a+2,3)=max(f(a+3,2),2−6)=max(f(a+3,2),−4).
  4. Fourth Call: f(a+3,2)=max(f(a+4,1),6−4)=max(0,2)=2 (base case for f(a+4,1).

Step 3: Backtrack to Get the Result

  1. f(a+3,2)=2, so f(a+2,3)=max(2,−4)=2.
  2. f(a+2,3)=2, so f(a+1,4)=max(2,3)=3.
  3. f(a+1,4)=3, so f(a,5)=max(3,−2)=3.

Final Answer: The value printed by the program is: 3.

Answer is 10230.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2015 | Set 3 | C Programming | Pointers | Question 16
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program.

C 
# include 
int main( )
{
  static int a[] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
  static int *p[] = {a, a+3, a+4, a+1, a+2};
  int **ptr = p;
  ptr++;
  printf("%d%d", ptr - p, **ptr};
}

The output of the program is _________
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Analyze the Arrays and Pointers - Array a[]: a = {10,20,30,40,50} - a[0]=10, a[1]=20, a[2]=30, a[3]=40, a[4]=50.
- Pointer Array p[]: Each element in p[] is a pointer pointing to specific elements in a[]: p[0] = a, p[1] = a+3 , p[2] = a+4, p[3] = a+1, p[4] = a+2.
- So: - p[0]=&a[0] (value = 10),
- p[1]=&a[3] (value = 40),
- p[2]=&a[4] (value = 50),
- p[3]=&a[1] (value = 20),
- p[4]=&a[2] (value = 30).
- Pointer to Pointer ptr: - Initially, ptr = p (i.e., ptr points to the first element of p[])
Step 2: Perform the Operations - Increment ptr: ptr++: Now ptr points to p[1], which is a+3 (i.e., the address of a[3]).
- Evaluate ptr−p: - ptr−p: The difference in addresses between ptr and p gives the index of ptr in p[].
- Since ptrptrptr now points to p[1]p[1]p[1], ptr−p=1ptr - p = 1ptr−p=1.
- Evaluate ∗∗ptr: - ∗∗ptr: Dereferencing ptr twice gives the value at the address p[1].
- p[1] = a + 3, and the value at a+3 is a[3] = 40.
Step 3: Final Output The output is: 140
Explanation:
- The first number (ptr−p) is 111.
- The second number (∗∗ptr) is 40.

    • a[0]=10, a[1]=20, a[2]=30, a[3]=40, a[4]=50.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2003 | Discrete Mathematics | Logic | Question 72
questions.question:
The following resolution rule is used in logic programming.
Derive clause (P ∨ Q) from clauses (P ∨ R), (Q ∨ ¬R) Which of the following statements related to this rule is FALSE?
questions.question_html:
The following resolution rule is used in logic programming.

Derive clause (P ∨ Q) from clauses (P ∨ R), (Q ∨ ¬R)

Which of the following statements related to this rule is FALSE?

options.html: ((P ∨ R) ∧ (Q ∨ ¬R)) ⇒ (P ∨ Q) is logically valid
(P ∨ Q) ⇒ ((P ∨ R)) ∧ (Q ∨ ¬R)) is logically valid

options.text: (P ∨ Q) is satisfiable if and only if (P ∨ R) ∨ (Q ∨ ¬R) is satisfiable
options.html: (P ∨ Q) is satisfiable if and only if (P ∨ R) ∨ (Q ∨ ¬R) is satisfiable

options.text: (P ∨ Q) ⇒ FALSE if and only if both P and Q are unsatisfiable
options.html: (P ∨ Q) ⇒ FALSE if and only if both P and Q are unsatisfiable

questions.explanation:
Option A: According to the resolution principle, (P+R)(Q+R’) -> (P+Q), hence this is correct.
Option B: (P+Q) -> (P+R)(Q+R’) = P’Q’ + PQ + P’R’ + QR. This is the converse of the Resolution Principle and the converse function does not work in Resolution Principle.
Hence, this is false, and therefore Option B is correct.
Option C: Any formula can be satisfiable only if at least one true exists in its truth table.
Consider here:
α = P + Q and β = (P + R)(Q + R’)
For α to be satisfiable, we must have P = 1, and Q = 1, then in that case β is also satisfiable.
For β to be satisfiable, we must have P = 1, Q = 0, and R = 0, then in that case, α is also satisfiable.
Therefore, the statement in option C is also valid.
Option D: Consider here,
Let us take α = (P+Q) as false and β: both P and Q are unsatisfiable.
α is true when only when P = false and Q = false, otherwise false in every case.
β is true only when P = false and Q = false and otherwise false in every case.
This implies that α ≣ β.
Hence, the statement in Option D is also correct.
Conclusion: As the question is asking which option is not correct, Hence Option B is the correct answer.
questions.explanation_html:
Option A: According to the resolution principle, (P+R)(Q+R’) -> (P+Q), hence this is correct.

Option B: (P+Q) -> (P+R)(Q+R’) = P’Q’ + PQ + P’R’ + QR. This is the converse of the Resolution Principle and the converse function does not work in Resolution Principle.
Hence, this is false, and therefore Option B is correct.

Option C: Any formula can be satisfiable only if at least one true exists in its truth table. 
Consider here:
α = P + Q and β = (P + R)(Q + R’)
For α to be satisfiable, we must have P = 1, and Q = 1, then in that case β is also satisfiable.
For β to be satisfiable, we must have P = 1, Q = 0, and R = 0, then in that case, α is also satisfiable.
Therefore, the statement in option C is also valid.

Option D: Consider here,
Let us take α = (P+Q) as false and β: both P and Q are unsatisfiable.
α is true when only when P = false and Q = false, otherwise false in every case.
β is true only when P = false and Q = false and otherwise false in every case.
This implies that α ≣ β. 
Hence, the statement in Option D is also correct.

Conclusion: As the question is asking which option is not correct, Hence Option B is the correct answer.

I. A programming language which does not permit global variables of any
kind and has no nesting of procedures/functions, but permits recursion
can be implemented with static storage allocation
II. Multi-level access link (or display) arrangement is needed to arrange
activation records only if the programming language being implemented
has nesting of procedures/functions
III. Recursion in programming languages cannot be implemented with dynamic
storage allocation
IV. Nesting of procedures/functions and recursion require a dynamic heap
allocation scheme and cannot be implemented with a stack-based allocation

questions.question_html:
Which of the following are true? 

I. A programming language which does not permit global variables of any
   kind and has no nesting of procedures/functions, but permits recursion 
   can be implemented with static storage allocation
II. Multi-level access link (or display) arrangement is needed to arrange 
    activation records only if the programming language being implemented 
    has nesting of procedures/functions
III. Recursion in programming languages cannot be implemented with dynamic 
     storage allocation
IV. Nesting of procedures/functions and recursion require a dynamic heap 
    allocation scheme and cannot be implemented with a stack-based allocation
    scheme for activation records
V. Programming languages which permit a function to return a function as its 
   result cannot be implemented with a stack-based storage allocation scheme 
   for activation records

options.html: I, III and IV only

questions.explanation:
I.  Recursion cannot be implemented with Static Storage Allocation.   Static allocation means, compiler has to decide size for function calls.  In case of recursion, it is not possible for compiler to decide as depth of recursion depends on recursion parameter which may be an input from user also. II. Is CORRECT.  Programming languages that support
nested subroutines also have a field in the call frame that points to the stack frame of the
latest
activation of the procedure that most closely encapsulates the callee, i.e. the immediate
scope
of the callee. This is called an
access link
or
static link
(as it keeps track of static nesting during dynamic and recursive calls) and provides the routine (as well as any other routines it may invoke) access to the local data of its encapsulating routines at every nesting level. Some architectures, compilers, or optimization cases store one link for each enclosing level (not just the immediately enclosing), so that deeply nested routines that access shallow data do not have to traverse several links; this strategy is often called a "display"  [Source:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Call_stack ] III. Recursion
CAN
be implemented with any kind of  Dynamic Storage Allocation scheme. IV. Nesting features are always implemented in a language using STACK and NOT Heap. (See above point II for details) V. Is CORRECT.  In stack based allocation scheme, once a function has returned, it is removed from function call stack.  Therefore returning a function from a function doesn't look possible.

questions.question_html:
Consider the function func shown below:

C 
int func(int num)
{
    int count = 0;
    while (num)
    {
        count++;
        num >>= 1;
    }
    return (count);
}

The value returned by func(435)is __________.

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2004 | C Programming | Functions | Question 33
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program segment:

C 
char p[20];
char *s = "string";
int length = strlen(s);
int i;
for (i = 0; i < length; i++)
     p[i] = s[length — i];
printf("%s",p);

The output of the program is (GATE CS 2004)
questions.explanation_html:
This code snippet appears to have a couple of issues that might prevent it from functioning as intended. Let's break it down:


```c

char p[20];

char *s = "string";

int length = strlen(s);

int i;

for (i = 0; i < length; i++)

p[i] = s[length - i];

printf("%s", p);

```


1. **String Termination**:

- The array `p` is not null-terminated. After the loop, there's no explicit null character (`'0'`) placed at the end of `p`. Therefore, `p` is not treated as a proper C string. This can lead to undefined behavior when printing `p`.


2. **Indexing Issue**:

- In the loop, `p[i]` is assigned `s[length - i]`. The indexing logic seems incorrect. It should be `s[length - i - 1]` to correctly copy the characters in reverse order. Additionally, it should be `length - i - 1` instead of `length - i` to prevent accessing out-of-bounds memory.


3. **Array Size**:

- The array `p` is declared with a size of 20, which is more than enough to store the reversed string "gnirts" (which is only 6 characters long). However, there's no check to ensure that the length of `s` does not exceed the size of `p`. If `s` were longer than 20 characters, it would result in a buffer overflow.


Here's the corrected version of the code:


```c

#include <stdio.h>

#include <string.h>


int main() {

char p[20];

char *s = "string";

int length = strlen(s);

int i;

for (i = 0; i < length; i++)

p[i] = s[length - i - 1]; // Correcting the indexing

p[length] = '0'; // Adding the null terminator

printf("%s", p);

return 0;

}

```


With these corrections, the code should correctly print the reverse of the string "string", which is "gnirts".

options.text: Recursion requires the activation record for the recursive function to be saved on a different stack before the recursive function can be called.
options.html: Recursion requires the activation record for the recursive function to be saved on a different stack before the recursive function can be called.

questions.explanation:
The portion of the stack used for an invocation of a function is called the function’s stack frame or activation record. An activation record is another name for Stack Frame. It's the data structure that composes a call stack. It is generally composed of:
- Locals to the callee
- Return address to the caller
- Parameters of the callee
The Call Stack is thus composed of any number of activation records that get added to the stack as new subroutines are added, and removed from the stack (usually) as they return. In C language the program execution starts with main function, so it is the first activation record in the function stack. Now looking at each option-
:
Option a
-
The statement is false as any number of functions can be called from the main. It might not be necessary that at-most one activation record exist and activation record of main. For example- as in case of recursion.
Recurse (int n)
{
if(n==0)
return ;
else
recurse(n-1);
}
In above example activation record of main and recurse(4) will have activation record of recurse(5), recurse(6) (more than one) if we pass n=6 initially to the function.
Option b
- True as soon as a function is called its activation record is created in the function stack.
Option c
- In C language variables are statically scoped not dynamically, the global variables are statically assigned address space when the execution starts not depending on when and where they are used.
Option d
- The functions are stored in the same each time recursive call is made, considering the above recurse function
Recurse(4) Recurse(5) Recurse(6) Main()   Refer
http://geeksquiz.com/c-misc-question-1/ This solution is contributed by
P
arul
sharma.

Consider the following two-dimensional array D in the C programming language, which is stored in row-major order:

int D[128][128];

Demand paging is used for allocating memory and each physical page frame holds 512 elements of the array D. The Least Recently Used (LRU) page-replacement policy is used by the operating system. A total of 30 physical page frames are allocated to a process that executes the following code snippet:

for (int i = 0;  i <  128;  i++) 
for (int j = 0; j < 128; j++)
D[j][i] *= 10;

The number of page faults generated during the execution of this code snippet is
questions.explanation:

int D[128] [128] Page frame holds 512 elements of the array D. A total 30 physical page
frames are allocated to a process that executes the following code snippet.
for (int i = 0, i < 128; i++)
for (int j = 0; j < 128; j++)
D[j] [i]* = 10;
for each i, the loop executes 128 times.
IMG Since 30-page frames were allocated to the process.
Each physical frame holds 512 elements.
And array access is in column-major order .
D[j] [i] * = 10
The first 4 rows belong to one page.
Since memory contains 30 page frames, and array contains 32 pages.
Page required for array = 128/4=32
No. of page fault = 128×32 = 4096

- Second: g(10) (prints 10) during parameter evaluation.
- Third: g(10) (prints 10) inside f(x,y).
- Fourth: h(20) (prints 20) inside f(x,y).

Hence the correct option is (A).

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2025 | Set 1 | Operating System | Process | Question 29
questions.question_html:
Suppose in a multiprogramming environment, the following C program segment is executed. A process goes into I/O queue whenever an I/O related operation is performed. Assume that there will always be a context switch whenever a process requests for an I/O, and also whenever the process returns from an I/O. The number of times the process will enter the ready queue during its lifetime (not counting the time the process enters the ready queue when it is run initially) is _______. (Answer in integer)

int main()

{

int x=0, i=0;
scanf("%d",&x);
for(i=0; i<20; i++)

{

x = x+20;
printf("%d\n",x);

}

return 0;

}
questions.explanation:
Program Analysis The program performs the following steps:
- Input operation: scanf("%d", &x); (I/O operation).
- Loop: Executes 20 iterations, each involving: - A computation: x = x + 20;
- An output operation: printf("%d\n", x); (I/O operation).
- Termination: return 0; .
Context Switches - A context switch occurs whenever the process requests an I/O operation or returns from an I/O operation.
- Each I/O operation causes the process to: - Leave the CPU and enter the I/O queue .
- Return from the I/O queue and re-enter the ready queue .
Counting Context Switches - Initial execution: - The process starts in the ready queue (not counted).
- First I/O operation: scanf - The process requests I/O and enters the I/O queue .
- After completing I/O, it re-enters the ready queue .
- Count = 1 .
- Loop iterations (20 times): - Each iteration involves a printf (I/O operation).
- For each printf : - The process requests I/O and enters the I/O queue .
- After completing I/O, it re-enters the ready queue .
- Count = 20 .
- Termination: - No I/O operation is performed here, so no context switch occurs.
Total Context Switches - Total times the process enters the ready queue (excluding the initial entry): - From scanf : 1
- From printf : 20
- Total = 1 + 20 = 21 .
questions.explanation_html:
Program Analysis

The program performs the following steps:

  1. Input operation: scanf("%d", &x); (I/O operation).
  2. Loop: Executes 20 iterations, each involving:
    • A computation: x = x + 20;
    • An output operation: printf("%d\n", x); (I/O operation).
  3. Termination: return 0;.

Context Switches

  • A context switch occurs whenever the process requests an I/O operation or returns from an I/O operation.
  • Each I/O operation causes the process to:
    1. Leave the CPU and enter the I/O queue.
    2. Return from the I/O queue and re-enter the ready queue.

Counting Context Switches

  1. Initial execution:
    • The process starts in the ready queue (not counted).
  2. First I/O operation: scanf
    • The process requests I/O and enters the I/O queue.
    • After completing I/O, it re-enters the ready queue.
    • Count = 1.
  3. Loop iterations (20 times):
    • Each iteration involves a printf (I/O operation).
    • For each printf:
      • The process requests I/O and enters the I/O queue.
      • After completing I/O, it re-enters the ready queue.
    • Count = 20.
  4. Termination:
    • No I/O operation is performed here, so no context switch occurs.

Total Context Switches

  • Total times the process enters the ready queue (excluding the initial entry):
    • From scanf: 1
    • From printf: 20
    • Total = 1 + 20 = 21.

questions.question_html:
#include <stdio.h>

int foo(int S[],int size){

  if(size == 0) return 0;

  if(size == 1) return 1;

  if(S[0] != S[1]) return 1+foo(S+1,size-1);

  return foo(S+1,size-1);

}

int main(){

int A[]={0,1,2,2,2,0,0,1,1};

  printf("%d",foo(A,9));

  return 0;

}

The value printed by the given C program is _______ . (Answer in integer)
questions.explanation:
Let's analyze the given C program step by step:
Function foo : The function foo takes an array S[] and its size as arguments. The function is recursive, and it returns:
- 0 if the size of the array is 0.
- 1 if the size of the array is 1.
- It checks if the first two elements ( S[0] and S[1] ) are different. If they are, it adds 1 to the result of the recursive call with the array excluding the first element ( S+1 ), and the size reduced by 1.
- If S[0] is equal to S[1] , it just makes a recursive call without adding anything.
Main Function: The array A[] is initialized with the values {0, 1, 2, 2, 2, 0, 0, 1, 1} , and the foo function is called with this array and its size 9 .
Recursive Call Analysis: - First call : foo(A, 9) - S[0] = 0 , S[1] = 1 → These are different, so we add 1 and call foo(S+1, 8) .
- Second call : foo(S+1, 8) where S = {1, 2, 2, 2, 0, 0, 1, 1} - S[0] = 1 , S[1] = 2 → These are different, so we add 1 and call foo(S+1, 7) .
- Third call : foo(S+1, 7) where S = {2, 2, 2, 0, 0, 1, 1} - S[0] = 2 , S[1] = 2 → These are the same, so we don't add anything and call foo(S+1, 6) .
- Fourth call : foo(S+1, 6) where S = {2, 2, 0, 0, 1, 1} - S[0] = 2 , S[1] = 2 → These are the same, so we don't add anything and call foo(S+1, 5) .
- Fifth call : foo(S+1, 5) where S = {2, 0, 0, 1, 1} - S[0] = 2 , S[1] = 0 → These are different, so we add 1 and call foo(S+1, 4) .
- Sixth call : foo(S+1, 4) where S = {0, 0, 1, 1} - S[0] = 0 , S[1] = 0 → These are the same, so we don't add anything and call foo(S+1, 3) .
- Seventh call : foo(S+1, 3) where S = {0, 1, 1} - S[0] = 0 , S[1] = 1 → These are different, so we add 1 and call foo(S+1, 2) .
- Eighth call : foo(S+1, 2) where S = {1, 1} - S[0] = 1 , S[1] = 1 → These are the same, so we don't add anything and call foo(S+1, 1) .
- Ninth call : foo(S+1, 1) where S = {1} - Size is 1, so the function returns 1 .
Returning the results: - Ninth call returns 1 .
- Eighth call returns 1 (no additional increment).
- Seventh call returns 1 + 1 = 2 .
- Sixth call returns 2 (no additional increment).
- Fifth call returns 2 + 1 = 3 .
- Fourth call returns 3 (no additional increment).
- Third call returns 3 (no additional increment).
- Second call returns 3 + 1 = 4 .
- First call returns 4 + 1 = 5 .
Final Answer: The value printed by the program is 5 .

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2025 | Set 1 | C Programming | Functions | Question 63
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program:

#include <stdio.h>

int gate (int n) {

  int d, t, newnum, turn;

  newnum = turn = 0; t=1;

  while (n>=t) t *= 10;

  t /=10;

  while (t>0) {

  d = n/t;

  n = n%t;

  t /= 10;

  if (turn) newnum = 10*newnum + d;

  turn = (turn + 1) % 2;

  }

  return newnum;

}

int main () {

 printf ("%d", gate(14362));

 return 0;

}

The value printed by the given C program is _______ . (Answer in integer)
questions.explanation:
Let's analyze the given C program to determine the value printed.
C Program Breakdown: - The function gate(int n) : - This function takes an integer n and performs some operations on it to return a new number ( newnum ).
- The variable t represents the place value (10's, 100's, etc.) and is used to extract the digits of n one by one.
- The turn variable controls whether we add the extracted digit ( d ) to the new number ( newnum ).
- The digits are taken in pairs: the first digit is discarded, and the second digit is added to newnum .
- How it works : - The while (n >= t) loop finds the highest power of 10 ( t ) that is less than or equal to n .
- The next while (t > 0) loop extracts the digits of n one by one.
- On each iteration, the digit d is extracted by d = n / t , and the number n is updated to remove the digit by n = n % t .
- The digit is added to newnum only when turn is 1 , and turn alternates between 0 and 1 .
Detailed Execution for gate(14362) : - Initially, n = 14362 , t = 1 , and turn = 0 .
- The first loop while (n >= t) is used to find the largest power of 10 less than or equal to 14362: - t = 10 , t = 100 , t = 1000 , t = 10000 (stopping here because 10000 is the largest power of 10 ≤ 14362).
- Now, the program begins the second while (t > 0) loop: - First iteration : - d = n / t = 14362 / 10000 = 1 (first digit is 1).
- n = n % t = 14362 % 10000 = 4362 .
- t = t / 10 = 1000 .
- Since turn = 0 , the digit d is not added to newnum . turn changes to 1 .
- Second iteration : - d = n / t = 4362 / 1000 = 4 (second digit is 4).
- n = n % t = 4362 % 1000 = 362 .
- t = t / 10 = 100 .
- Since turn = 1 , newnum = 10 * newnum + d = 0 * 10 + 4 = 4 . turn changes to 0 .
- Third iteration : - d = n / t = 362 / 100 = 3 (third digit is 3).
- n = n % t = 362 % 100 = 62 .
- t = t / 10 = 10 .
- Since turn = 0 , the digit d is not added to newnum . turn changes to 1 .
- Fourth iteration : - d = n / t = 62 / 10 = 6 (fourth digit is 6).
- n = n % t = 62 % 10 = 2 .
- t = t / 10 = 1 .
- Since turn = 1 , newnum = 10 * newnum + d = 4 * 10 + 6 = 46 . turn changes to 0 .
- Fifth iteration : - d = n / t = 2 / 1 = 2 (fifth digit is 2).
- n = n % t = 2 % 1 = 0 .
- t = t / 10 = 0 .
- Since turn = 0 , the digit d is not added to newnum . turn changes to 1 .
- The loop ends because t = 0 , and the function returns newnum , which is 46 .
Final Answer: The value printed by the given C program is 46 .

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2025 | Set 2 | C Programming | Loops & Conditionals | Question 33
questions.question_html:
int x=126,y=105;

do {

if(x>y) x=x-y;

else y=y-x;

} while(x!=y);

printf("%d",x);

The output of the given C code segment is ________. (Answer in integer)
questions.explanation:
Initially, x = 126 and y = 105. The loop continues as long as x ≠ y, and in each iteration, it subtracts the smaller value from the larger one.
In the first iteration: Since x > y, x = x - y = 126 - 105 = 21.
Now x = 21 and y = 105. In the next iteration: y = y - x = 105 - 21 = 84.
The process continues until both values become equal, and at that point, the loop ends. After the loop, both x and y will be 21, which is the GCD of 126 and 105

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2025 | Set 2 | C Programming | Pointers | Question 62
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program:

#include

int main(){

int a;

int arr[5] = {30,50,10};

int *ptr; ptr = &arr[0] + 1;

a = *ptr; (*ptr)++; ptr++;

printf("%d", a + (*ptr) + arr[1]);

return 0; }

The output of the above program is ___________. (Answer in integer)
questions.explanation:
arr[5] = {30, 50, 10, 0, 0} . The array is initialized with values 30, 50, and 10, the rest are 0.
ptr = &arr[0] + 1; sets ptr to point to arr[1] (value 50).
a = *ptr; sets a = 50 .
(*ptr)++; increments arr[1] from 50 to 51.
ptr++; moves ptr to arr[2] (value 10).
a + (*ptr) + arr[1] becomes:
50 + 10 + 51 = 111

questions.title: GATE | CS | 2025 | Set 2 | C Programming | Functions | Question 63
questions.question_html:
Consider the following C program:

#include

int g(int n) {

return (n+10);

}

int f(int n) {

return g(n*2);

}

int main() {

int sum, n;

sum=0;

for (n=1; n<3; n++)

sum += g(f(n));

printf ("%d", sum);

return 0;

}

The output of the given C program is ________. (Answer in integer)
questions.explanation_html:
For n = 1:

  • f(1) calls g(1 * 2) → g(2).
  • g(2) returns 2 + 10 = 12.
  • sum += g(12) → g(12) = 12 + 10 = 22.
  • sum = 22.

For n = 2:

  • f(2) calls g(2 * 2) → g(4).
  • g(4) returns 4 + 10 = 14.
  • sum += g(14) → g(14) = 14 + 10 = 24.
  • sum = 22 + 24 = 46.

Thus, the final value of sum is 46
